g.data-narration: Welcome to Function Calls in ARM Microcontrollers. This topic covers the essential mechanisms for modular programming in embedded systems.
g.text: Understanding AAPCS, BL Instructions & Stack Frames

g.data-narration: Function calls enable modular code in microcontrollers, allowing reusable subroutines that save memory and improve organization.
g.text: Why Function Calls Matter in Embedded Systems
• Modular, reusable code instead of repetitive logic
• Critical for ISR handling, RTOS tasks, and bare-metal code
• ARM Cortex-M uses BL instruction for function calls

g.data-narration: We will explore AAPCS register conventions, the BL instruction mechanics, and stack frame management for nested calls.
AAPCS defines R0-R3 for arguments (caller-saved), R4-R11 as callee-saved, and special registers SP, LR, PC for control flow.
g.text: ARM Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS) - Register Usage
Can be modified by called function
Stack pointer, return address, program counter

g.data-narration: First four arguments pass in R0-R3; more use the stack. Return values use R0 (32-bit) or R0-R1 (64-bit).
The Branch with Link (BL) instruction branches to target while saving the return address in LR (R14).
Function prologue saves registers (PUSH) and allocates stack space. Epilogue restores registers and returns to caller.
g.text: Alternative: BX LR (simple return without restoring)

g.data-narration: A stack frame contains return address, saved registers, local variables, and outgoing arguments for nested calls.
In a simple call, arguments go in R0-R1, BL saves return address, function computes result in R0, returns via BX LR.
Nested calls require saving LR in prologue. The inner function call would overwrite LR without saving it first.
g.text: PUSH {R4, LR} ; Save R4 and return address
ADD R4, R0, R1 ; Local var = a + b
MOV R0, R4 ; Pass to square()
⚠ Always save LR for nested calls!

g.data-narration: When functions have more than 4 arguments, the first four use R0-R3, and the fifth+ arguments are passed via the stack.
g.text: MOV R0,#2 MOV R1,#3 MOV R2,#4 MOV R3,#5 BL multiply_add

g.data-narration: In ARM, the stack grows downward from higher to lower addresses. SP is decremented before each push operation.
Key exam points: R0-R3 are caller-saved, R4-R11 are callee-saved. Always save LR in prologue for nested calls.
Avoid: forgetting LR save for nested calls, using B instead of BL, and modifying callee-saved registers without saving.
g.text: ✗ Forgetting to save LR → nested call corrupts return address

g.data-narration: In summary: AAPCS governs register usage, BL enables function calls, and proper prologue/epilogue ensures correct nested execution.
g.text: BL = Branch + Link (saves return in LR) • BX LR = Return to caller
Prologue: PUSH {regs, LR} • Epilogue: POP {regs, PC} or BX LR
